coredata.dc:title: Direct photodissociation of toluene molecules to photoluminescent carbon dots under pulsed laser irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Zhifeng
Wang, Shengda
Chang, Yajing
Yu, Dabin
Jiang, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a facile method of direct photodissociation of toluene molecules to photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) under pulsed laser irradiation in the absence of surfactants or catalysts. The as-synthesized CDs with diameters 1.3–4.0 nm present regular emission peaks and the crystallographic structure of these species has been identified as graphite 2H from the analysis of high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Raman spectrum. In addition, First-Principle calculations were performed to discuss the photon-induced excitation in the photodissociation process. The results demonstrate that electrons can be transited from Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) to Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) in toluene molecule by 248 nm single photon excitation process and the Stark effect plays a crucial role in the photodissociation process. These CDs exhibit excellent stable emission due to the stability of crystal structure, which could be considered for promising luminescent applications.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants from the Open Research Fund of State Key Laboratory of Pulsed Power Laser Technology of Electronic Engineering Institute in Hefei ( SKL 2015 KF 04 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61076040 ), and the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (No. 2012011111006 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon Dots (CDs) synthesized by photodissociation of toluene molecules. Inset: photographs of toluene liquid before/after Laser irradiation and photoluminescence of CDs under 365 nm UV–light. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelength from 335 to 380 nm; (b) Emission peaks of PL spectra from (a), note that different PL spectra have similar emission peaks, (c) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the CDs obtained after one year, (d) Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the CDs under 370 nm excitation, 3.84 ns for 415 nm emission and 3.94 ns for 435 nm emission. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Raman spectrum of CDs by 532 nm Laser irradiation, Inset: Gaussian fitting Curve from 2200 to 3200 cm−1, two peaks corresponding to the 2D and D + G mode; (b) XPS survey scan of the CDs on silicon; (c) XPS high resolution survey of C 1s.

HRTEM images: (a) Dispersed CDs, Inset: the statistical distribution of CDs in (a) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) of single dots in (a), (b–d) Carbon dot (CD) with space distance 0.343 nm, 0.210 nm and 0.204 nm, (e–g) Images observed at

A schematic illustration of photodissociation: Calculated Density of States (DOS) of toluene molecule. Single photon excitation process for electrons transited from HOMO to LUMO and multiphoton excitation processes for photodissociation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Space distances and related interface angles.

head.ce:title: Direct photodissociation of toluene molecules to photoluminescent carbon dots under pulsed laser irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Anhui, 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pulsed Power Laser Technology, Electronic Engineering Institute, Anhui, 230037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulsed Power Laser Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Anhui, 230009, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a facile method of direct photodissociation of toluene molecules to photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) under pulsed laser irradiation in the absence of surfactants or catalysts. The as-synthesized CDs with diameters 1.3–4.0 nm present regular emission peaks and the crystallographic structure of these species has been identified as graphite 2H from the analysis of high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Raman spectrum. In addition, First-Principle calculations were performed to discuss the photon-induced excitation in the photodissociation process. The results demonstrate that electrons can be transited from Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) to Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) in toluene molecule by 248 nm single photon excitation process and the Stark effect plays a crucial role in the photodissociation process. These CDs exhibit excellent stable emission due to the stability of crystal structure, which could be considered for promising luminescent applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials have a broad range of applications in energy, environmental and biomedical fields [1–6]. In particular, photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) including graphene dots (GDs) have received considerable interests due to their excellent photoluminescent properties and promising applications [7–16]. For the synthesis approaches, CDs can be fabricated via several processes including electrochemical exfoliation [7,8], chemical process [9–11], microwave irradiation [14], hydro-thermal method [16,17], etc. For example, the electrochemically generated CDs [7], by cutting of graphite rods in NaOH/EtOH electrolyte, exhibit fluorescence with different emission colors: blue, green, yellow, and brown, which can be used for degradation reaction of methyl blue (MB) with the composite of CDs and TiO2/SiO2. The electrochemically generated GDs [8], by exfoliating the graphite anode in NaOH aqueous solution and after reduction with hydrazine, emit strong yellow photoluminescence, which can be used for direct stem cell labeling. The chemically prepared CDs, by carbonizing citric acid or glucose, do not show photoluminescence at the initial steps, but exhibit bright blue fluorescence after the passivation by 1-hexadecylamine (HDA) [9,10] and hydrazine [11], etc. The passivation is an effective way to get photoluminescent CDs, which are also found in the laser assisted fabrication of CDs, such as poly-(ethylene glycol) (PEG) passivation [12,13]. Different from these approaches, microwave pyrolysis provides a one-step method to synthesize photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) by using different amounts of precursors including PEG and saccharides [14]. The hydrothermal method synthesized nitrogen-doped photoluminescent CDs, by using ammonium citrate precursor show high fluorescence quantum yield up to about 13.5%, which can be applied as pH sensors and biomedical sensors, etc. [17]. The hydrothermal method prepared GDs, by using the polythiophene derivatives (PT2) as the carbon source, exhibit a broad absorption in the UV–visible region and excellent 1O2 generation capability, which can be applied as a photodynamic therapy (PDT) agent for the simultaneous imaging and highly efficient treatment of cancer [16]. However, little attempt was focused on the investigation of the crystal structure of these CDs. Therefore, searching for synthesizing high-quality photoluminescent CDs remains a great challenge.

These reported chemical synthesis methods can be classified into two types, called “top-down” and “bottom-up” strategies. Despite these chemical approaches, pulsed laser techniques, considered as physical synthesis methods, have been used to fabricate a wide range of nanomaterials [18–22]. Recently, pulsed laser have been employed to synthesize photoluminescent CDs [12,13,23–25]. For the “top-down” strategy, CNPs [12], produced by a 1064 nm pulsed Nd:YAG laser ablation of a carbon target, exhibit different emission colors with different excitation wavelengths after the surface passivation. Alternatively, the “bottom-up” synthesized GDs [23], irradiated by the Nd:YAG laser on a suspension of nickel oxide (as catalyst) in benzene, show blue PL in ethanol, methanol and water, which can be used to mark bacterial cells. Most recently, the Nd:YAG laser with a wavelength of 1064 nm was used to irradiated the toluene solution and non-fluorescent graphene sheets were generated at 5 min. The graphene sheets were finally ablated into small fluorescent CDs at 10 min [25]. These laser assisted approaches are valid methods to generate high quality photoluminescent CDs, however, the question remains how the photons contribute activation in the process of surface passivation [13] or achieve molecular dissociation in the synthesis of CDs [23].

In this study, for the first time, we present a facile method of direct photodissociation of toluene molecules to photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) under pulsed laser irradiation in the absence of surfactants or catalysts. Different from the ablation of graphene [25], the as-synthesized photoluminescent CDs with sizes of 1.3–4.0 nm exhibit regular emission peaks. The crystallographic structures were carefully analyzed from high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images including the Fast Fourier transform (FFT) images and HRTEM simulations. The results have identified these CDs as graphite 2H structured dots, which agree well with the experiment data from Raman spectra. During the course of this investigation, the photodissociation processes of toluene molecule have been discussed. Theoretical calculations have demonstrated that electrons can be transited from Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) to Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) in toluene molecule by single photon excitation process, which is discussed later in some detail.

Toluene (>99.5%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). The COM-PexPro 102 Laser system used for irradiation was manufactured by Lambda Physik (America), a KrF laser system producing maximum energy about 400 mJ/pulse. The centrifuge generator Multifuge X3R was manufactured by Thermo Scientific.

75 ml toluene was filled in a quartz beaker. The liquid was directly irradiated for 30 min by the pulsed laser operated at 248 nm with energy density of 170 mJ/pulse and duration of 20 ns at 10 Hz. After irradiation, the transparent liquid turned to light yellow, as shown in Fig. 1 . The CDs solution emits blue-green luminescence under 365 nm UV-light irradiation, which is extremely stable even laid aside for one year (see photograph in Fig. 1). It is worth noting that the dissociate process was not the pyrometric aftereffect of laser, as the product in the solution became black carbon particles while the beam focused in the solution from the side of the quartz beaker. The reason was that quartz transforms about 15% of photon energy into thermal energy.

The solution together with CDs was first centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, leaving the precipitate in the bottom. Then, the precipitate was dried in order to remove the toluene and about 6 mg CDs were obtained. After that, the dried precipitate was mixed with 10 ml methanol and ultrasonic shook for 15 min. Finally, the CDs were dissolved in the supernatant liquid.

The HRTEM samples were prepared by dripping the CDs methanol solution on ultra-thin holey carbon films and dried at room temperature. The Raman and XPS samples were prepared by dripping the CDs methanol solution on silicon wafer covered by a dish and dried in the cabinet at 60°, which was performed for several times.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of CDs were measured by Shimadzu UV2550. The PL spectra were then recorded by Hitachi F-4600. The time-resolved fluorescence (TRF) spectra were measured at room temperature by HORIBA Scientific time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique under Nanosecond LED 370 nm. The Raman spectra were carried on LabRam HR eVolution under 532 nm laser excitation. The HRTEM images and EDS spectra of CDs were collected by JEOL JEM-2100 F. The XPS experiments were acquired with Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi operated with Al Kα source (1486.6 eV), 500 mm diameter analyzer.

Optical properties of CDs were measured to reveal effect of PL properties. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of CDs show a wide range of light from 400 nm to 270 nm with a maximum absorption peak at 283 nm and a narrow full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of 25 nm, which is similar to that of the CDs synthesized by microwave approach [14]. The peak from 270 nm to 300 nm is ascribed to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [7,17,26], while the peaks at 335 nm is ascribed to the n–π* transition of the CO bond at the surface of CDs [17]. Other peaks at 345 nm, 355 nm, 365 nm and 380 nm present weaker absorption and excite visible blue light.

The PL spectra in Fig. 2 a show the redshift of each excitation wavelength, which is common in fluorescent carbon materials [7]. Different from the microwave synthesized CNPs [14] and hydrothermal prepared CDs [16], the PL spectra consist of a mixture of regular emission peaks. The Lorentzian fitting curves (Fig. S1) are employed to reveal the emission peak centers. Fig. 2b shows that the emission peaks are located at 370 nm, 390 nm, 414 nm, 440 nm, 472 nm and 500 nm. After one year, the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra (Fig. 2c) of the CDs were measured again and the emission peaks remain at 413 nm, 435 nm, 468 nm and 501 nm, which is similar to Fig. 2a. This indicates that the CDs exhibit extra-high stability, even after being kept for 1 year at room temperature.

The time-resolved fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2d) were measured to investigate the photoexcited carriers decays. The fluorescence lifetime of the PL was estimated by fitting a two-component exponential mixture model. The average fluorescence lifetime of these CDs was calculated to be 3.84 ns for 415 nm emission and 3.94 ns for 435 nm emission. Typically, photoluminescence may derive from intrinsic state and defect state, including quantum size effect, zig-zag, armchair sites. Compared with the fluorescence lifetime of Graphene Quantum dots (GQDs) [27], these short lifetime emission can be classified as intrinsic state emissions. Therefore, the regular PL peaks might originate from the specific diameters of CDs rather than the surface defects [17,23], which is also found by silicon nanocrystals and GQDs [7,28]. The less surface defects could be ascribed to the rich atomic hydrogen around the CDs during the photodissociation of toluene molecules. Accordingly, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized CDs was 8.7% by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution (54%) as the standard, which is similar to the QY of the GQDs [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman and XPS spectra of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Raman spectra have been widely used in the study and characterization of carbon materials [29–35], which provide valuable information about the hybrid of CC bond including graphene, graphite, nanodiamonds, etc. Science the CDs present fluorescence behavior under the 532 nm laser irradiation during the measurement of Raman spectrum, the baseline was fitted by LabSpec and subtracted from the Raman spectrum. The Raman peaks at 1350 cm−1 (D mode) and 1575 cm−1 (G mode) correspond to Graphene or graphite while the peak at 1332 cm−1 agree with diamonds [29,30]. The peak at 1575 cm−1 is related to E2g mode of optical branches around Γ, since the motion of carbon atoms is restricted to the plane of graphite crystals, while the peak at 1350 cm−1 is attributed to breathing mode A1g arising from sixfold ring in the micro/nano crystalline graphite clusters near the K zone boundary [29,31,32]. The Raman spectra of CDs in this work present the significant dominant peaks at 1348.4 cm−1 (D mode) and 1577.2 cm−1 (G mode), as shown in Fig. 3 a. A slight frequency shift toward higher wavenumber about 2.5 cm−1 can be found due to the extremely small crystal size [32]. The inset shows a broad peak around 2800 cm−1. The Gaussian fitting curve presents a peak around 2700 cm−1 related to 2D mode [35] and 2900 cm−1 to D + G mode.

The XPS measurements were performed for the surface elemental analysis of the CDs. Fig. 3b shows that the presence of C, N and O in the as-synthesized CDs, where the peak of C 1s is stronger than that of N 1s and O 1s. The high resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 3c) of C 1s is centered at 284.8 eV corresponding to CC/CC, which is consistent with Raman result.

The structure of a crystal can be determined by analysis of HRTEM and electron diffraction patterns. Fig. 4 a shows that the CDs are well dispersed with diameter from 1.3 to 4.0 nm and based on carbon material. However, the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) of single dot reveals the presence of carbon and copper. The signal of copper may be excited from the mesh of copper while the signal of carbon is rising from the ultra-thin holey carbon film together with CDs. Fig. 4b–j have confirmed that the CDs are stacked in a hexagonal crystal structure corresponding to graphite 2H (PDF 89–7213/ICSD #76,767) [36,37] with space group P63/mmc (unit cell lengths a = 2.464 Å c = 6.711 Å). The smallest unit cell contains four atoms. In single crystals of graphite, the shortest interatomic distance is 1.42 Å in the plane, while the distance between the adjacent planes is 3.354 Å. The first space distance 0.343 nm in Fig. 4b should be the (002) planes of graphitic carbon, which is similar to the CDs synthesized from other groups [3,7,16,38]. However, the distance is a bit larger than that of the graphite 2H crystal. The second space distance 0.210 nm, shown in Fig. 4c, is likely to appear in many relative orientations of the symmetry zone axes:
( 0
1 ¯
10 )
plane of graphite 2H tilted along
< 10
1 ¯
1 ¯
>
,
( 1
1 ¯
00 )
plane along
< 11
2 ¯
1 ¯
>
,
( 01
1 ¯
0 )
plane of graphite 2H tilted along < 20
2 ¯
1 ¯
> , etc. Similarly, the space distance 0.213 nm, corresponding to
( 01
1 ¯
0 )
plane of graphene, was found in the HRTEM images of GDs [23,39]. However, single layer graphene, a very thin layer material shown as hexagonal lattice in plane with distance about 0.213 nm along
< 0001 >
zone axes, would cause very poor contrast even on the ultra-thin holey carbon film. Thus, the structure in Fig. 4c could not be graphene but graphitic carbon, which has also been proved by the HRTEM (Fig. 4 e–j) and Raman spectrum. The third space distance 0.204 nm, shown in Fig. 4d, is also likely to appear in many relative orientations of the symmetry zone axes:
(
1 ¯
10
1 ¯
)

<
1 ¯
102 >
and
( 0

)
plane along
<

21
2 ¯
>
zone axes, etc.

Fig. 4e–g shows the two rhombic arrays that differ in the direction of their major axis. These patterns show significant differences from Fig. 4b-d, which can be made a careful survey of space distances and the interface angles. The space distances 0.209 nm and 0.202 nm in Fig. 4e conform well with
( 0
1 ¯
10 )
and
(
1 ¯
01
1 ¯
)
planes at
[ 10
1 ¯
1 ¯
]
direction. The interface angle θ between
( 0
1 ¯
10 )
and
(
1 ¯
01
1 ¯
)
in Fig. 4e is about 61.9°, which is very close to the calculated interface angle 61.54° (see Table 1 ). The space distances 0.203 nm and 0.185 nm in Fig. 4f correspond to

( 10

)

[ 2

11 ]
direction. The interface angle between
( 01

and
( 10

)
is about 75.9°, while the calculated interface angle is 76.13° (Table 1). Fig. 4g shows the space distances 0.203 nm and 0.199 nm agree well with
(
1 ¯

and
( 0

)

[ 1
1 ¯
01 ]
direction. The interface angle 69.6° between
(
1 ¯
011 )
and
( 0
1 ¯
1
1 ¯
)
is very close to the calculated angle 68.75° (Table 1).

The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns have been usually recorded to investigate the crystal structure of material and single crystal with spots array can be used to determine the zone axis and orientation relations [40]. Since the CDs are much smaller than the selected area aperture (∼100 nm), the SAED pattern would not show spots array but spots around circles (see Fig. 1 in Supplementary). Then, the FFT images have been transformed from the HRTEM images. Fig. 4h–j are FFT images transformed from Fig. 4e–g and each pattern has determined two indices of lattice plane at the adjacent spots with zone axis. However, the
( 0
1 ¯
10 )
plane in Fig. 4h is not very clear. Hence simulated HRTEM images and electron diffraction patterns of graphite 2H at
[ 10
1 ¯
1 ¯
]
,
[ 2
1 ¯
11 ]
and
[ 1
1 ¯
01 ]
directions (Fig. 5 a-c) are employed to study the crystal structure of CDs. The simulated images (Fig. 5a-f) agree well with the experiment data (Fig. 4e-j). Besides, an atomic model for each projected direction is shown in the simulated HRTEM image (Fig. 5a-c). However, the focus situation is different from the defocus situation (see Fig. 2 in Supplementary) indicating that the atomic model does not always hold the dark dots in HRTEM image, while the electron diffraction pattern is not changed.

How does the photodissociation perform and how do electrons transit between the molecule orbitals (MO)? Then, First-Principle calculations by Density functional theory (DFT) were performed to find out the electron activity by photon excitations, as illustrated in Fig. 6 . The Density of States (DOS) of toluene molecule was calculated and the states were separated in several areas. The first is located at −269 eV and the second is from −21 eV to 14 eV, while there is nothing above 15 eV since electrons with such high energy could get free from the molecule. The energy level about −269 eV is the ground states containing the 1s orbitals in carbon atom. If such electron has been excited to 15 eV, the energy gap will be 284 eV, which confirms well with the experiment data, such as XPS and electron energy-loss spectroscopy (EELS). In the sp2 hybridized carbon materials, the binding energy of C1s in XPS is 285 eV [41–43], while the EELS gives much more subtle information, including 1s-π* anti-bond orbital at 285 eV and 1s-σ* anti-bond orbital at 291 eV [44–46].

Table 2 shows the electron occupation of different states associated with electronic orbitals in toluene molecule. The 14 electrons occupy 7C1s orbitals of 7 carbon atoms in toluene molecule around −269 eV. The states of Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) are −5.66 eV and −0.68 eV. The energy gap between HOMO and LUMO is 4.98eV.

In a Franck–Condon transition, the motion of the electron that is exchanged between electronic states is much faster than the nuclear rearrangement of relative separation [47]. The energy of single photon in this work is 5.0 eV (=248 nm), which is so close to the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO that the electrons in HOMO can be easily transited to LUMO by single photon excitation. Nevertheless, such excitation could not dissociate the toluene molecule, but multi-photon excitation processes would crack the aromatic rings. However, the other energy gaps may not be equal to 5.0 eV (Table 2). Since the laser beam is often polarized with a pulse duration (about 20 ns), Stark effect may excite the states into resonance and this may broaden the molecules energy levels [48–50], which causes the multi-photon excitation processes within very short time. As a result, the bonding in toluene molecule is annulled by promotion of an electron from a bonding or non-bonding orbital into an anti-bonding orbital. The molecule then dissociates along a repulsive potential surface [47,51]. The carbon photofragmentation then gather and self-assemble to CDs.

In summary, photoluminescent CDs with graphite 2H structure have been directly synthesized by photodissociation of toluene molecules under pulsed laser irradiation. The PL spectra show regular emission peaks with short fluorescence lifetime, indicating that these emission peaks may originate from the specific diameters of CDs. In addition, the photodissociation processes have been discussed by First-Principle calculations, demonstrating that electrons can be transited between Molecular Orbitals by single/multi-photon excitation, which furthermore achieve the photodissociation of toluene molecule and directly synthesize the CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the Open Research Fund of State Key Laboratory of Pulsed Power Laser Technology of Electronic Engineering Institute in Hefei (SKL 2015 KF 04), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61076040), and the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (No.2012011111006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.04.047.
